# Introduction {#sec-introduction}

```{r}
#| label: setup-01
#| include: false
library(countSFA)
```

Stochastic frontier analysis (SFA), introduced by @aigner1977 and @meeusenBroeck1977, is a workhorse of production-efficiency measurement in economics. It quantifies the gap between observed output and the maximum attainable given inputs by isolating a one-sided inefficiency term in the production frontier. The two founding papers restricted the one-sided disturbances to half-normal and exponential distributions, respectively. @greene1990 generalised the inefficiency term to a Gamma distribution, whose shape parameter accommodates an interior mode rather than a density pinned at zero. The Gamma generalisation in continuous-output SFA does not admit a closed-form likelihood. @greene1990 evaluates the required integrals by Laguerre quadrature, and @greene2003 later proposes a simulated-likelihood estimator. @greene1990 argues that the half-normal and exponential restrictions can distort the distribution of estimated firm inefficiencies, motivating the more flexible Gamma shape. This literature considers continuously distributed outputs and does not extend directly to settings in which the dependent variable is a non-negative integer count.

Count-valued production outcomes are common in applied work. Patents granted to a firm measure innovation output [@griliches1990; @hallGrilichesHausman1986]; hospital admissions and emergency-department visits measure healthcare utilisation; accident reports measure safety performance; and shipment counts measure trade activity. Such data are supported on the set of positive integers, exhibit substantial mass at zero, and are typically right-skewed. Applying a continuous frontier to a log-transformed count requires ad hoc adjustments, discards the discrete likelihood, and yields efficiency scores whose statistical properties are difficult to characterise.

@hoflerScrogin2008 and @feHofler2013 take the first steps toward a count-data frontier [see @fe2019, for a survey of SFA models with discrete outcomes]. @feHofler2013 specify $Y_i \mid u_i \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda_i e^{\mp u_i})$ with frontier mean $\lambda_i = \exp(\mathbf{x}_i'\boldsymbol{\beta})$ and a half-normal inefficiency $u_i \geq 0$, treating both the production ($-$) and cost ($+$) orientations in parallel. The model does not admit a closed-form likelihood, and parameters are recovered via simulated likelihood [see @feHofler2020, for a software implementation]. Subsequent extensions have addressed unobserved heterogeneity through finite mixtures [@drivasEconomidouTsionas2019] and endogeneity of the regressors via copula corrections [@haschkaHerwartz2022, among others], all retaining the half-normal inefficiency and the simulation-based likelihood. That assumption, however, fixes the mode of inefficiency at zero and inherits a symmetric bell shape from the underlying continuous draw. These features are better motivated by the continuous literature than by count-data applications. The half-normal framework also lacks a flexible shape parameter analogous to the Gamma generalisation of @greene1990, restricting practitioners to a single inefficiency shape regardless of the empirical context.

We propose a Poisson stochastic frontier in which the inefficiency term follows a Gamma distribution. We treat both production and cost frontiers in parallel through the conditional model

$$
Y_i \mid u_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i \;\sim\;
   \mathrm{Poisson}\!\left(\lambda(\mathbf{x}_i;\,\boldsymbol{\beta})\, e^{\mp u_i}\right),
\qquad
u_i \;\sim\; \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha,\, b),
$$

with shape $\alpha > 0$ and rate $b > 0$, where the upper sign defines the production frontier (e.g., patent counts, hospital discharges, or scientific publications) in which $\lambda_i$ is an upper bound on the conditional mean; and the lower sign defines the cost frontier (e.g., emissions, accidents, or defects) and inefficiency inflates the realised rate. The frontier mean $\lambda(\mathbf{x}_i;\,\boldsymbol{\beta}) > 0$ may take any positive form; for estimation we adopt the canonical log-linear specification $\lambda(\mathbf{x}_i;\,\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \exp(\mathbf{x}_i'\boldsymbol{\beta})$, although other specifications could be equally adopted.

For given $\alpha$, the marginal PMF admits an absolutely convergent alternating-series representation in the production orientation and a one-dimensional integral with bounded integrand in the cost orientation. In either case the conditional mean $\mathrm{E}[e^{-u_i} \mid Y_i = y_i,\, \mathbf{x}_i]$ that defines the firm-level technical efficiency score inherits the same structure.

Setting $\alpha = 1$ recovers the exponential inefficiency of @meeusenBroeck1977 and reduces both PMFs to closed-form expressions through the lower and upper incomplete gamma functions respectively, with parallel closed forms for the efficiency score. The exact (or adaptive-quadrature) likelihood evaluation eliminates the need for maximum simulated likelihood in either orientation. The incompleteness of the gamma function is the algebraic consequence of the one-sided constraint $u_i \geq 0$: relaxing it and integrating over $u_i \in \mathbb{R}$ replaces the incomplete gamma with the complete gamma and recovers the negative binomial, i.e., the standard overdispersion model of @cameronTrivedi2005 [Ch. 20].

We contribute to this literature in four directions. First, we derive the marginal PMF and conditional efficiency score under Gamma inefficiency, expressed as an absolutely convergent alternating series in the production frontier and a one-dimensional integral in the cost frontier. Second, we show that the exponential case reduces both to closed-form expressions through the lower and upper incomplete gamma functions respectively, eliminating the need for maximum simulated likelihood. Third, we develop a practical maximum likelihood estimator and extend the framework to include inefficiency determinants via a scaling model that preserves the closed-form likelihood; the cost-orientation likelihood relies on adaptive quadrature on the $v = e^{a + u}$ substitution but is otherwise equivalent. Finally, we apply the model to the @hausman1984 patent panel, the canonical benchmark for count-data econometrics, and show that the exponential frontier improves over the baseline Poisson GLM, that capital intensity and science-sector membership are statistically significant determinants of inefficiency, and that the estimated firm-level efficiency scores reveal substantial heterogeneity. All methods are implemented in the R package `countSFA` [@countSFApkg], distributed alongside this paper.

We validate the estimator in a Monte Carlo study covering both orientations. In the production frontier, bias and root-mean-squared error shrink at the parametric $\sqrt{n}$ rate, and the correctly specified exponential frontier attains nominal 95% coverage from $n = 500$ onward. We document a flat-$\alpha$-direction phenomenon under which the delta-method confidence interval for the Gamma shape parameter under-covers across all sample sizes, for which we recommend profile-likelihood intervals. The cost-orientation exponential frontier exhibits the same $\sqrt{n}$ convergence and near-nominal coverage despite the per-observation quadrature, and the Poisson GLM intercept inflation matches the predicted $\alpha\,\log\bigl(b/(b-1)\bigr)$ factor exactly.

The remainder of the paper is organised as follows. @sec-model derives the marginal PMF and conditional efficiency score for both production and cost frontiers under Gamma inefficiency, treating the exponential case as the closed-form $\alpha = 1$ corollary. @sec-existence characterises the unconditional and conditional moments, identifies the existence regions for the rate parameter, and develops the scaling extension to observed inefficiency determinants. @sec-estimation develops the maximum likelihood estimator, addresses numerical implementation, and discusses identification. @sec-simulations reports Monte Carlo evidence on finite-sample performance. @sec-empirical presents the patent application. @sec-conclusion summarises and outlines potential extensions. All the proofs are collected in the Appendix.
